America wouldn't have had the Manhattan project had it not been for George Paget Thomson, Chadwick and the collaboration of the British theoreticians and experimentalists Cockcroft, Peiers, Oliphant,   Tuck, Thomson and more, many more.  Chadwick, neutron discoverer and Nobel winner had his own section at Los Alamos, and he and Groves got on very well, in fact it was Chadwick (who had known Bohr from pre WW1 days in Manchester under Rutherford) had been the one instrumental in getting Bohr out of Sweden to Scotland and then out to Los Alamos.  I can only think Nolan literally based the film on the book he read and did no other research as to why these scientists weren't even name checked, on the principle that one should never assign to malice what could equally be attributed to incomptence.  But, if you are clear that Nolan isn't incompetent, however...

An antagonist like Strauss seems so meaningless to me in the greater context of what Oppenheimer means to the world. A real antagonist would have been someone who challenged Oppenheimer's determination to build a bomb to begin with, not someone like Strauss who thought more about his career and personal insults by Oppenheimer. Or it could have been Trueman, an antagonist later in Oppenheimer's life when he finished the bomb, but wouldn't want it to be used. Neither Trueman nor bomb critics got any real screentime.

Julius Robert Oppenheimer was a physicist and director of the Los Alamos laboratory during the development of the atomic bomb after the Nazi and Soviet invasion of Poland in 1939. He was selected to administrate the repertory carrying out the Manhattan project. This program developed the first generations of atomic weapons, with the first weapons being used at the end of the Second World War from 1939 to 1945. After his resignation in 1945 from the Manhattan project due to the destructive power of Little Man and Fat Boy, he became the chairman and general adviser to the committee of the Atomic Energy Commission also before the murder and assassination of Pres John F. Kennedy in 1963 Kennedy announced that Oppenheimer would be receiving Enrico Fermi award for his extraordinary achievement in physics. He was presented this reward by Lyndon B Johnson, president, after Kennedy’s assassination on December 1963.

Julius Robert but I’m was born in the city of New York to German and Jewish immigrants on April 22, 1904; after he graduated from the University of Harvard, he went to the English university of Cambridge, where he began his research into topics at the Cavendish Laboratory in 1925. During his time at Gottingen, he teamed up with Max Born; Niels Bohr received a doctorate at Gottingen while also developing what became known as the Born-Oppenheimer method, which was a contribution to quantum molecular theory. Oppenheimer died on 18th February 1967 of throat cancer.

Julius Robert Oppenheimer became politically active in the 1930s and agreed with Leo Szilard and Albert Einstein that Nazi Germany could potentially develop their own atomic weapons. With the invasion of Poland in 1939 by Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union, the Manhattan project was started for the United States to harness the potential of atomic weapons before their rivals of the Japanese Empire or the Third Reich/Nazi Germany built their own. Oppenheimer led the project from Los Alamos in New Mexico beginning in 1942. The project itself was full of scientists that fled Nazi Germany. Oppenheimer himself was descended from Jewish and German immigrants, making the matter of developing the first atomic weapon exceedingly personal with the bomb initially planned to attack Germany. Still, by 1944, it was apparent that the war in Europe was won, so the United States changed its plans to drop the atom bombs on the Japanese Empire due to the Empire being entrenched in the Japanese home islands and with the Japanese army successfully taking control and winning the war in China by 1945 if the bomb was not launched then the war could have continued easily until the late 1940s. The Manhattan project was initially given the funding of US $6000, with the budget accumulating to $2 billion by 1945.

My two cents:  DO.  NOT.  GO.  TO.  THIS.   MOVIE.   Nolan has made stylistic and editorial choices that prevent this movie from being engaging in the slightest.  You will not care about the characters.  You will not feel ANYTHING for them.  You will not care about their motivations.  Unless you already know the history, you will not know what is going on.  You will be frustrated by the CEASELESS cutting away from scenes where little or nothing has even happened yet.   You will be confused, then annoyed, then tortured by the score / soundscape which completely abandons the most basic tenet of performance art — the buildup and release of tension.  You will roll your eyes at the absurd use of psychedelic visual elements that add ZERO to your overall understanding / experience.  

Save your money, and do something better with your time.  Youtube has plenty of videos that will tell you the story of the creation of the bomb and Oppenheimer's persecution, and deliver the message of "nuke bombs bad" as effectively, or maybe even more effectively, than this film.

Edit:  Minutes after posting this, I got this sneaky suspicion that I wasn't harsh enough.  So to be perfectly clear:  If I had not gone with two of my friends, if I had instead gone by myslef, I absolutely would have gotten up at about the 45 minute point and walked out of this turd.  I stayed and suffered for the entire three hours of throbbing bullshit because I didn't want my friends to feel obligated to leave with me.

My dad is 95. He is an atomic vet. That means he was a similarly-recruited scientist (in the post Oppenheimer days) who studied the above-ground tests. The first time he told us kids about what it was like to really witness the bomb, I was in early high school, many, many years ago. He said, as a conclusion, if every leader would see it, they would destroy them all. He is a man of few words, but his words about what it was like to witness his team's test bombs are unforgettable. Today, he just had heart surgery. This week, after recovery, my brother plans to take him to see this movie. The only opinion I care about concerning this movie is his. I wonder how similar that discussion about this movie will be to the first discussion I had with him when he talked about the experience of his own. I haven't seen the movie yet, but I am hopeful my dad says they did it justice. Now, my decision, wait until I hear what my dad says, or go see it first?
Edit: Dad and my brother are going on August 16 to see the movie in 70mmIMAX. I saw it last Friday. I'll update after August 16, and at some point, video our dad. Thank you so much for this outpouring of support. I never anticipated it. Really, thank you.

Oppenheimer faced many challenges in his new role. He had to recruit and manage hundreds of scientists from different disciplines and backgrounds, who often had conflicting opinions and egos.

He had to deal with the technical difficulties and dangers of working with radioactive materials and explosives. He had to cope with the pressure and secrecy of a wartime project that could determine the fate of millions of lives.

But he also had a vision. He wanted to create a bomb that would work on the principle of implosion, where a sphere of plutonium would be compressed by a surrounding shell of conventional explosives, triggering a nuclear chain reaction. He believed that this method would be more efficient and reliable than the alternative gun-type design, where two pieces of uranium would be shot together to form a critical mass.

Oppenheimer assembled a team of experts to work on the implosion concept at Los Alamos, New Mexico, where he established a remote laboratory in 1943. He also collaborated with other scientists at different sites across the country, such as Enrico Fermi at Chicago, Ernest Lawrence at Berkeley, and Hans Bethe at Cornell. He coordinated their efforts and synthesized their ideas into a coherent plan.

He also faced some opposition and skepticism from his peers and superiors. Some doubted that the implosion method would work at all, or that it would be ready in time for the war. Some questioned his loyalty and security clearance, due to his past associations with communists and pacifists. Some resented his authority and influence over the project.

But Oppenheimer persevered and prevailed. He proved himself to be a capable leader and a creative thinker, who could inspire and motivate others with his eloquence and enthusiasm. He solved many technical problems and overcame many obstacles along the way. He also developed a strong sense of responsibility and morality for his work, which he expressed in poetic and philosophical terms.

He finally achieved his goal on July 16, 1945, when he witnessed the first successful test of an atomic bomb at Trinity Site in New Mexico.

He later recalled that he was reminded of a verse from the Hindu scripture Bhagavad Gita: "Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds." He realized that he had unleashed a new force of nature that could change history forever.

A few weeks later, two atomic bombs based on Oppenheimer's design were dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki in Japan, killing hundreds of thousands of people and ending World War II. Oppenheimer was hailed as a hero by some and vilified as a villain by others.

He himself was ambivalent about his role in creating such a destructive weapon. He later said: "In some sort of crude sense which no vulgarity, no humor, no overstatement can quite extinguish, the physicists have known sin; and this is a knowledge which they cannot lose."

After the war, Oppenheimer became an advocate for international control of nuclear power and disarmament. He opposed the development of the hydrogen bomb, which was even more powerful than the atomic bomb. He also clashed with some political and military factions who wanted to use nuclear weapons as instruments of intimidation and domination. As a result, he was accused of being disloyal and untrustworthy by a government committee in 1954, which revoked his security clearance and stripped him of his influence.

Oppenheimer spent the rest of his life as a professor at Princeton University's Institute for Advanced Study, where he continued to teach and research on physics. He also wrote and lectured on various topics related to science, culture, philosophy, and ethics. He received several honors and awards for his contributions to science and humanity, including the Enrico Fermi Award in 1963 from President John F. Kennedy.

He died in 1967 from throat cancer at age 62. He left behind a legacy that is both admired and controversial, both celebrated and criticized. He was one of the most influential figures in modern history who shaped our understanding of nature's secrets and our ability to harness them for good or evil. He was Robert Oppenheimer: The father of the atomic bomb.

There are several books about J. Robert Oppenheimer that are considered to be excellent. One of the most well-known and highly respected is "American Prometheus: The Triumph and Tragedy of J. Robert Oppenheimer" by Kai Bird and Martin J. Sherwin. This biography covers Oppenheimer's life in great detail, including his work on the Manhattan Project and his later years as a controversial figure in the world of science and politics. Another highly recommended book is "Oppenheimer: The Tragic Intellect" by Charles Thorpe. This book provides a psychological and philosophical examination of Oppenheimer's life and work.

Not long after the fall of Norway in 1940, British intelligence learned that the Nazis had begun accelerating their diabolical atomic weapons program. The Norwegian hydroelectric plant at Vemork, which was the world's largest heavy water mass-production site, was an essential element for the Germans to progress with their nuclear weapons programme.

The British launched 3 commando operations to disrupt the production of heavy water;

Operation Grouse - 4 Norwegians were trained to a ludicrously high level by the British in Scotland, once the 4 Commandos were ready they were dropped into the wilderness of Norway, where they survived by being total Bear Grylls level badasses. They carried out reconnaissance and gathered vital information, and prepared for the British to send a substantial commando raid.

Operation Freshman - 34 British commandos were sent to destroy the heavy water plant when it became apparent that the Germans were close to developing a nuclear reactor. The elite 34 man team was to fly in on gliders and link up with the Norwegians, to hit the Germans hard and destroy the heavy water plant.

The entire operation was a tragic disaster, a storm caused chaos, one glider and its tow plane crashed into a mountain with all commandos and pilots lost and the other glider crashed with injuries. All the survivors were eventually found by the Germans and executed on Hitler's commando order.

Operation Gunnerside - February 1943, after the previously failed operation, the British remained relentless in the mission to knock out the Nazi nuclear weapons project. Six Norwegians were trained to specialist commando level and then parachuted in to link up with the original Grouse team, which was still surviving like badasses.

The 9 men skied to the heavy water plant, then descended into a deep ravine, forded the treacherous icy river and then climbed a cliff face. Before sneaking past the substantial German guard force, and into the hydro plant.

Once inside they set about planting shed loads of explosives on the critical heavy-water electrolysis chambers. The bombs detonated and the entire inventory of heavy water produced during the German occupation was destroyed, along with the machinery to produce more. The Norwegian Commandos all escaped, skiing 300 odd kilometres to safety in Sweden.

The Norwegian squad commander told his team ahead of the mission;

“I cannot tell you why this mission is so important, but if you succeed, it will live in Norway’s memory for a hundred years.” — Norwegian Royal Army Colonel Leif Tronstad.

After the stunningly successful raid, it turned out the fiendish Germans had a stockpile of heavy water ready to transport in a depo near the hydro plant, the Germans loaded it onto a ferry and were set to send it across a vast lake, on its journey to Germany, where it would have been enough to carry out Hitler's nuclear program.

Only another 3 Norwegians were like ‘frack that’ and they snuck onto the ferry, planting a massive explosive charge. The ferry with its valuable cargo was sunk, sending Hitler's dream of nuclear weapons to the bottom of a lake.

“How close were the Nazis to developing an atomic bomb? The truth is that National Socialist Germany could not possibly have built a weapon like the atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima or Nagasaki. This was not because the country lacked the scientists, resources, or will, but rather because its leaders did not really try.”

“They were certainly trying to win the war. And they were willing to devote huge amounts of resources to building rockets, jet planes, and other forms of deadly and sometimes exotic forms of military technology. So why not the atomic bomb? Nazi Germany, it turns out, made other choices and simply ran out of time.”

“In January of 1939, the German chemists Otto Hahn and Fritz Strassmann published the results of an historic experiment: after bombarding uranium with neutrons—neutrally charged particles—they found barium, an element roughly half the size of uranium. Their former colleague Lise Meitner, who a few months before had been forced to flee Germany and seek refuge in Sweden, and her nephew Otto Frisch realized that the uranium nucleus had split in two. These revelations touched off a frenzy of scientific work on fission around the world.”

“The German "uranium project" began in earnest shortly after Germany's invasion of Poland in September 1939, when German Army Ordnance established a research program led by the Army physicist Kurt Diebner to investigate the military applications of fission. By the end of the year the physicist Werner Heisenberg had calculated that nuclear fission chain reactions might be possible. When slowed down and controlled in a "uranium machine" (nuclear reactor), these chain reactions could generate energy; when uncontrolled, they would be a "nuclear explosive" many times more powerful than conventional explosives.”

During the last months of the war, a small group of scientists working in secret built and tested a nuclear device.

“Whereas scientists could only use natural uranium in a uranium machine, Heisenberg noted that they could use pure uranium 235, a rare isotope, as an explosive. In the summer of 1940, Carl Friedrich von Weizsícker, a younger colleague and friend of Heisenberg's, drew upon publications by scholars working in Britain, Denmark, France, and the United States to conclude that if a uranium machine could sustain a chain reaction, then some of the more common uranium 238 would be transmuted into "element 94," now called plutonium. Like uranium 235, element 94 would be an incredibly powerful explosive. In 1941, von Weizsícker went so far as to submit a patent application for using a uranium machine to manufacture this new radioactive element.”

“Researchers knew that they could manufacture significant amounts of uranium 235 only by means of isotope separation. At first German scientists led by the physical chemist Paul Harteck tried thermal diffusion in a separation column. In this process, a liquid compound rises as it heats, falls as it cools, and tends to separate into its lighter and heavier components as it cycles around the column. But by 1941 they gave up on this method and started building centrifuges. These devices use centripetal force to accumulate the heavier isotopes on the outside of the tube, where they can be separated out. Although the war hampered their work, by the fall of the Third Reich in 1945 they had achieved a significant enrichment in small samples of uranium. Not enough for an atomic bomb, but uranium 235 enrichment nonetheless.”

“Uranium machines needed a moderator, a substance that would slow down the neutrons liberated by chain reactions. In the end, the project decided to use heavy water—oxygen combined with the rare heavy isotope of hydrogen—instead of water or graphite. This was not (as one of the many myths associated with the German nuclear weapons effort had it) because of a mistake the physicist Walther Bothe made when he measured the neutron absorption of graphite. Rather, it appeared that the Norsk Hydro plant in occupied Norway could provide the amounts of heavy water they needed in the first stage of development at a relatively low cost.”

“The Norwegian resistance and Allied bombers eventually put a stop to Norwegian production of heavy water (see Norwegian Resistance Coup and See the Spy Messages. But by that time it was not possible to begin the production of either pure graphite or pure heavy water in Germany. In the end, the German scientists had only enough heavy water to conduct one or two large-scale nuclear reactor experiments at a time.”

Heisenberg and his colleagues did not push as hard as they could have to make atomic bombs.

“By the very end of the war, the Germans had progressed from horizontal and spherical layer designs to three-dimensional lattices of uranium cubes immersed in heavy water. They had also developed a nuclear reactor design that almost, but not quite, achieved a controlled and sustained nuclear fission chain reaction. During the last months of the war, a small group of scientists working in secret under Diebner and with the strong support of the physicist Walther Gerlach, who was by that time head of the uranium project, built and tested a nuclear device.”

“At best this would have been far less destructive than the atomic bombs dropped on Japan. Rather it is an example of scientists trying to make any sort of weapon they could in order to help stave off defeat. No one knows the exact form of the device tested. But apparently the German scientists had designed it to use chemical high explosives configured in a hollow shell in order to provoke both nuclear fission and nuclear fusion reactions. It is not clear whether this test generated nuclear reactions, but it does appear as if this is what the scientists had intended to occur.”

“All of this begs the question, why did they not get further? Why did they not beat the Americans in the race for atomic bombs? The short answer is that whereas the Americans tried to create atomic bombs, and succeeded, the Germans did not succeed, but also did not really try.”

“This can best be explained by focusing on the winter of 1941-1942. From the start of the war until the late fall of 1941, the German "lightning war" had marched from one victory to another, subjugating most of Europe. During this period, the Germans needed no wonder weapons. After the Soviet counterattack, Pearl Harbor, and the German declaration of war against the United States, the war had become one of attrition. For the first time, German Army Ordnance asked its scientists when it could expect nuclear weapons. The German scientists were cautious: while it was clear that they could build atomic bombs in principle, they would require a great deal of resources to do so and could not realize such weapons any time soon.”

“Army Ordnance came to the reasonable conclusion that the uranium work was important enough to continue at the laboratory scale, but that a massive shift to the industrial scale, something required in any serious attempt to build an atomic bomb, would not be done. This contrasts with the commitment the German leadership made throughout the war to the effort to build a rocket. They sunk enormous resources into this project, indeed, on the scale of what the Americans invested in the Manhattan Project.”

“Thus Heisenberg and his colleagues did not slow down or divert their research; they did not resist Hitler by denying him nuclear weapons. With the exception of the scientists working on Diebner's nuclear device, however, they also clearly did not push as hard as they could have to make atomic bombs. They were neither heroes nor villains, just scientists working on weapons of mass destruction for Hitler's Germany.”

1. In Hinduism there are three categories of knowledge or understanding. They are 'Agyan' meaning ignorance, 'Sugyan' meaning right knowledge and 'Viparith Gyan' meaning perverted understanding. So, Hinduism preaches that, for the right understanding, a Guru or teacher is a must. Bhagavad-Gita is a treatise that gives a different understanding with every reading. Three universal teachers of Hinduism ie.,  Shankaracharya, Ramanujacharya and Madhwacharya have given different interpretations of the holy book. Such is the deep meaning of the content of the book. Someone can't just pick a copy of it,  read and claim to have understood it. Oppenheimer just did this. He didn't even have a spiritual Guru. He didn't ever pray God or practice any faith in his life. This says it all about him. 
2. He claims himself to be the destroyer of the world, by quoting Bhagavad-Gita. Is this belief not found in Christianity? What about the belief of ' Day of last judgement' then? Did he not read Bible? 

No matter what Oppenheimer had meant, his quoting of a verse from Bhagavad-Gita in the context of his heinous act and propagating it sends wrong perception of Hinduism to the world and  deeply hurts the sentiments of Hindus, who are essentially tolerant and peace-loving community. 
Om shanthi. Amen.

This might be an unpopular opinion, but for anybody who saw Oppenheimer in 70mm IMAX like I did, were you expecting a little more from the bomb drop scene when they tested? Like it looked great n all and every moment leading up to the detonation was extremely nerve wracking, suspenseful, but me personally.. I was just expecting a lot more sound from the explosion rather than it just being silent for a minute and then that sudden burst of aftershock. Don’t get me wrong, I still think this film is a masterpiece and will easily going down as one of the greatest motion pictures ever, but that one scene just felt like it should’ve been SO MUCH MORE BOOMING.. still peak tho.

It's ok, I actually agree with you on the sound there being a little underwhelming.  For an explosion that big, I think it should be a bit more of a gradual rumble than a bang, needs to be more "big" than "loud" but it was just a sudden, loud bang.  Felt kind of incongruous with the visuals.  Of course I've never seen a nuke test, or even a regular bomb or a cannon, but I've seen plenty of fireworks, and to me it's awe-inspiring to hear the thunderous rumble echo off the trees and terrain.  If mere fireworks sound like that, how much more so a nuke.
I very much appreciated that they showed a realistic time before the explosion and you hearing it, rather than the normal way of hearing the explosion with the sound regardless of distance.
In true meaning it's the best film must watch. World is not prepared but chillings ongoing 😄

Mediocre movie for me.. just because it is Nolan directed doesnt mandate it to be a good movie..

Main issue was - there was no coherence in the movie.. throughout the 180min it felt like Mr Nolan just glued together some scenes.. background music seemed unnecessary at certain points ..

More focus on showing politics in the movie as opposed to showing more around scientific stuffs - which is why i feel the thumbnail on the trailer is misdirecting audience.. the thumbnail gives a vibe that the movie will be about some hifi scientific stuffs whereas it was more about the politics around Trinity and Manhattan project..

And finally i am a fan of non-linear movies but only when it is done properly.. Nolan did a very poor job of the non-linearity in this movie.. too many abrupt scene switch and that happened for 3 hours..

As someone known to often be critical of films, especially in this day and age of rampant remakes and sequels, this was a breath of fresh air to an industry I was concerned had gone stale. Understand that when I say this, I do not do so lightly and only with the confirmation of others with far more technical knowledge of the craft than I: This movie may be one of the greatest films of all time. It’s a difficult thing to imagine when one thinks about it, but understand that when I say this, I mean it. If you have not seen it, I urge you to do so in the best settings possible, as soon as possible. Choose a theater near you with the best possible sound system, see it on the best screen possible, etc. It’s a film that should be seen in theaters… and it will stay with you long after the performance has come to its climactic conclusion.

Watched it today and it is a typical Christopher Nolan masterpiece. 

You need some level of intelligence to understand what’s going on because even when dumbed down to the bare basics, it’s still a lot to take in. I’m not talking about the physics, which has wisely been left to a small portion of the film. But the morality of it all. 

If you are a peace loving hippy, you will hate this film. If you are a Gung-ho ‘nuke em all’ person, you will hate this film. The film does not debate the existence of nuclear weapons, or the encouragement for bigger and better. But merely the limitation and control of nuclear weapons.

I saw it last night and it was long, slow, boring, and over-rated. I appreciate sophisticated movie plots but this is not for your average viewer as it will put you to sleep in the first hour (which it drags on for three btw). 

It's not just about the atom bomb, it's about Oppenheimer's connection with the Communist party, investigations, hearings, and how he lost his security clearance. I understood the complexity of the movie I just didn't find it interesting. You need to have a very deep understanding of who he was and what happened back in the day and if you don't then you're going to need to a shot of espresso to stay awake. 

The acting was great, Oppenheimer was a remarkable person, but I don't jump on the critic's bandwagon who rave about something as such. It's deep with lots of layers sure, but you still have to make it palatable and interesting to watch. Nolan is definitely talented but therein lies where he lacks greatly.
Attention span of a 3 year old.
I agree with this comment entirely. I've just seen it. I found it long, slow and profoundly boring.

@jagaloon216 right and I think most people agree with that but since it's getting rave reviews no one wants to admit that. 

You can have a complex storyline with layers of deep content but it still needs to be interesting to watch all the way through. That's the difficult part when making a film that's almost nothing but dialogue. It's like listening to someone talk to you for three hours. Sooner or later you're going to get bored.

@inutero3516   it has nothing to do with that. I watch movies with complex plots all the time and I love something that's deep with a historical significance especially. I just didn't find it that interesting. Are you a suck up who jumps on the bandwagon just because a bunch of critics raved about it and you don't want to look stupid?

One of the approaches that I follow is not to watch the trailer, avoid articles (news or leisure), avoid social media and any piece of information related to the movie at all costs so that at the end of the day, at theatres, I know I am about to witness something mind blowing and every penny is gonna be worth it. THAT. That feeling is astonishing. 

And mind you everyone reading this, even though you've watched the trailer (it's now after watching the movie that I'm watching it), you certainly shouldn't miss out on this masterpiece. Unless you have a home theatre, for which you'll still have to wait and avoid spoilers, concluding, GO Watch the film.

And for once try to follow my way of watching blockbusters. Every second and every dime will feel worth it.

Oppenheimer and his acolytes may not have known then all that their terrifying creation wreaked, but we do today, including the fact that, according to journalist John Hershey’s 1946 nonfiction account, Hiroshima, shadows were literally blasted from presumably incinerated human beings and imprinted on walls, like ghostly murals. Unfortunately, Nolan pulls his punches and cops out by never explicitly showing what the bombing of Hiroshima and Nagasaki cost in human terms. Unlike the Trinity test in the empty desert, the horrifying explosions in Japan are alluded to but never depicted per se in this movie.

FACTS about J. Robert Oppenheimer :
[ "After the war, Oppenheimer chaired the U.S. Atomic Energy Commission. He opposed developing an even more powerful hydrogen bomb. When President Truman finally approved it, Oppenheimer did not argue, but his initial reluctance and the political climate turned against him. In 1953, at the height of U.S. anticommunist feeling, Oppenheimer was accused of having communist sympathies, and his security clearance was taken away. He had, in fact, had friends who were communists, mostly people involved in the antifascist movement of the thirties. This loss of security clearance ended Oppenheimer's influence on science policy. He held the academic post of director of the Institute of Advanced Study at Princeton, and in the last years of his life, he thought and wrote much about the problems of intellectual ethics and morality. He died of throat cancer in 1967. "]

In a world where the power to annihilate lies within humanity's grasp, the atomic bomb serves as a grim reminder of the destructive potential we hold. Across nations, the possession of these weapons has triggered an alarming arms race, raising concerns about the future of global security.

As we bear witness to rising tensions and the development of more powerful weapons, it becomes imperative to unite as a global community, advocating for peace and working towards a world free from the menace of nuclear warfare.

The recent events in Russia and Ukraine serve as stark reminders of the devastating consequences of armed conflicts and territorial disputes. The war-torn region faces untold human suffering, loss of innocent lives, and vast destruction of infrastructure. These tragic events underscore the urgent need for peaceful resolutions and diplomatic dialogue.

In the face of adversity, let us stand united in calling for an end to hostilities and a return to the negotiating table. We must prioritize global cooperation, understanding, and empathy over violence and hatred. By fostering a culture of dialogue and reconciliation, we can pave the way for lasting peace and mutual respect.

It is time for us to reflect on the lessons of history and make a collective commitment to being advocates of peace. We must be instruments of change, encouraging our leaders to prioritize diplomacy and peaceful means to resolve conflicts.

As we aspire towards a world where nuclear weapons are but a distant memory, we must remember that the path to peace is not a solitary journey. It requires collective efforts and unwavering determination to safeguard the future of our planet and all its inhabitants.

Let us raise our voices together, embracing the vision of a peaceful world, where wars are replaced with cooperation, understanding, and compassion. The road to peace may be arduous, but with perseverance and unity, we can forge a brighter future, free from the shadow of the atomic bomb.

In solidarity for peace and humanity,

Just saw it and I’m here to say if you haven’t seen it, go watch it. My heart starts racing even when I start to think about some of the scenes. I read a comment somewhere that said “the film keeps building and building until the end where it doesn’t disappoint” and I 100% agree. 

If you are looking for an action movie with little to no plot don’t watch it. 

If your looking for a comedy, don’t go watch it. 

If your looking for one of the most important stories in the history of the world that was done in a way that could not have been more intense and engaging, go watch it. 

The one criticism that I found see as fair is that the movie was complex and there is a lot of moving parts and characters and I can see how you could get lost. 

With that being said, a story as complex as Oppenheimer’s could not have been made more digestible (and entertaining) as it was so you have to keep that in mind. Also personally I find this as a positive because I get to go watch it again and pick up some more gems that I wasn’t able to the first time. 

Just my take on this film. ✌️😄

Saw it last night; the first movie I’ve seen in the theater in 3 1/2 years…. Was worth the wait; there are definitely some sounds that you can feel viscerally experiencing it on the big screen with a good sound system.  Several people left after about 15 minutes; can’t blame them as if you don’t have some basic interest in physical science (and the history of it), the beginning was a bit dry and academic.  Was it a little long? Yes, but still worth powering through.  Cillian Murphy KILLED this performance!!

I agree, Murphy (and Nolan) totally nailed it and it was an excellent, excellent film.
As for those who left in 15 minutes, I might venture a guess that they "knew their limits": This film is not for the faint.  I think the film was intended to be watchable by an attentive moviegoer without prior knowledge of Oppenheimer or physics.  The technical details just didn't factor heavily into the plot, it was dumbed down enough that you could follow.  For example- two types of bombs, with different core materials and mechanisms, here's how much we have of each (jars).  It's not for the faint because you need a lot of focus (and dare I say above average intellect) just to be able to follow, understand, and synthesize the nuances and thematic elements.  Have to lock onto the dialogue and juggle the juxtaposed timeframes.  I'd say the sound levels were the only weak point as I often struggled to hear the dialogue over the music in the less dramatic scenes.
If you're an average phone-groomed teen with a short attention span (or an average adult looking for a quick easy flick), and you walk in because you just heard it was "really good" and had bewbs, you'll quickly realize _Oppenheimer_ isn't for you.  If you can keep it together though, you can experience the sheer excellence.
I never felt like I had to "power through", I was stuck in my seat because it took all my attention and kept rewarding it.  The more you can focus the better the film gets.  Even the beginning had my attention because I just sensed the academic parts would play into the plot development.
A lot of the comments seem to talk about the importance of seeing this in a big theatre, IMAX if practicable.  I saw it in a regular one with good seats up close, but my opinion is that if you truly "get" the film then the visual experience is mere icing on the cake.  I've seen other visuals as good as this, but the storytelling, acting, and screenplay are what make it exceptional.  If you can watch it on a phone with reasonable headphones and still be blown away, you "get" the film.

This story was very deep, I recently watched it this morning and the names still mess me up but this story has a great plot. 

!Spoiler Alert!
In a scene, Oppenheimer goes to the White House to meet the president. The president was proud of his invention but expected more higher power technology. In a part Oppenheimer said that he feels like he has bloody hands because even though America won and his nuclear bomb was theoretically successful, he felt guilty and ashamed. The president quickly got mad and said that nobody even gives a hecc of who created the bomb, more so, who threw the bomb. The intention of creating the bomb was to scare Japan for them to surrender. But the president decided to actually throw the bomb at them, killing over 70k people I think. 
Then in another scene you can see in 2:14, the citizens of America was waving the American flag, happy that Japan was gone, Oppenheimer’s hearing of the crowd went blurry. His vision went white, it was all his illusion. He felt so ashamed of himself, he killed so many people. He saw somebody’s flesh peeling off, melting away. Then stepping into someone’s ashes. 
Fast forward to a meeting, I forgot who it was but he started asking him questions, he wasn’t trying to do a nice little questionnaire, he started saying stuff like: “you knew this was gonna happen if you create that bomb, you knew this would happen, why did you create the bomb then, huh?” the questions overwhelmed Oppenheimer. Intense music arrived. He was flooded with the questions, he couldn’t even listen anymore. Suddenly, the questions stopped. Back home, Oppenheimer’s wife was mad. She asked him why he would let *the persons name* torment him. 

this whole movie was worth the 3 hours, it told so much and the names were so confusing and the scenes went by so quickly. It was truly a masterpiece, it did a great job projecting the actual World War II situation. 10/10, would recommend.

For all the idiot people who disagree with using the atomic bomb, this is for you. 

“I was one of a small group of scientists and engineers interrogating an intelligent, well-informed Japanese Army officer in Yokohama. We asked him what, in his opinion, would have been the next major move if the war had continued. He replied: "You would probably have tried to invade our homeland with a landing operation on Kyushu about November 1. I think the attack would have been made on such and such beaches."

"Could you have repelled this landing?" we asked, and he answered: "It would have been a very desperate fight”
What would have happened then?" we asked.

“He replied: "We would have kept on fighting until all Japanese were killed, but we would not have been defeated," by which he meant that they would not have been disgraced by surrender.”

“It is easy now, after the event, to look back and say that Japan was already a beaten nation, and to ask what therefore was the justification for the use of the atomic bomb to kill so many thousands of helpless Japanese in this inhuman way; furthermore, should we not better have kept it to ourselves as a secret weapon for future use, if necessary? This argument has been advanced often, but it seems to me utterly fallacious.

“From this background I believe, with complete conviction, that the use of the atomic bomb saved hundreds of thousands—perhaps several millions—of lives, both American and Japanese; that without its use the war would have continued for many months; that no one of good conscience knowing, as Secretary Stimson and the Chiefs of Staff did, what was probably ahead and what the atomic bomb might accomplish could have made any different decision. Let some of the facts speak for themselves.”

“Was the use of the atomic bomb inhuman? All war is inhuman. Here are some comparisons of the atomic bombing with conventional bombing. At Hiroshima the atomic bomb killed about 80,000 people, pulverized about five square miles, and wrecked an additional ten square miles of the city, with decreasing damage out to seven or eight miles from the center. At Nagasaki the fatal casualties were 45,000 and the area wrecked was considerably smaller than at Hiroshima because of the configuration of the city.”

“Compare this with the results of two B-29 incendiary raids over Tokyo. One of these raids killed about 125,000 people, the other nearly 100,000.”

“Of the 210 square miles of greater Tokyo, 85 square miles of the densest part was destroyed as completely, for all practical purposes, as were the centers of Hiroshima and Nagasaki; about half the buildings were destroyed in the remaining 125 square miles; the number of people driven homeless out of Tokyo was considerably larger than the population of greater Chicago. These figures are based on information given us in Tokyo and on a detailed study of the air reconnaissance maps. They may be somewhat in error but are certainly of the right order of magnitude.”

“Was Japan already beaten before the atomic bomb? The answer is certainly "yes" in the sense that the fortunes of war had turned against her. The answer is "no" in the sense that she was still fighting desperately and there was every reason to believe that she would continue to do so; and this is the only answer that has any practical significance.”

J. Robert Oppenheimer, in full Julius Robert Oppenheimer, (born April 22, 1904, New York, New York, U.S.—died February 18, 1967, Princeton, New Jersey), American theoretical physicist and science administrator, noted as director of the Los Alamos Laboratory (1943–45) during development of the atomic bomb and as director of the Institute for Advanced Study, Princeton (1947–66). Accusations of disloyalty led to a government hearing that resulted in the loss of his security clearance and of his position as adviser to the highest echelons of the U.S. government. The case became a cause célèbre in the world of science because of its implications concerning political and moral issues relating to the role of scientists in government.

Oppenheimer was the son of a German immigrant who had made his fortune by importing textiles in New York City. During his undergraduate studies at Harvard University, Oppenheimer excelled in Latin, Greek, physics, and chemistry, published poetry, and studied Eastern philosophy. After graduating in 1925, he sailed for England to do research at the Cavendish Laboratory at the University of Cambridge, which, under the leadership of Lord Ernest Rutherford, had an international reputation for its pioneering studies on atomic structure. At the Cavendish, Oppenheimer had the opportunity to collaborate with the British scientific community in its efforts to advance the cause of atomic research.

Julius Robert Oppenheimer (/ˈɒpənˌhaɪmər/; April 22, 1904 – February 18, 1967) was an American theoretical physicist and director of the Los Alamos Laboratory during World War II. He is often credited as the "father of the atomic bomb" for his role in organizing the Manhattan Project, the research and development undertaking that created the first nuclear weapons.

Born to German Jewish immigrants in New York City, Oppenheimer earned a bachelor's degree in chemistry from Harvard University in 1925 and a PhD in physics from the University of Göttingen in Germany in 1927. After research at other institutions, he joined the physics department at the University of California, Berkeley, where he became a full professor in 1936. He made significant contributions to theoretical physics, including achievements in quantum mechanics and nuclear physics such as the Born–Oppenheimer approximation for molecular wave functions, work on the theory of electrons and positrons, the Oppenheimer–Phillips process in nuclear fusion, and the first prediction of quantum tunneling. With his students, he also made contributions to the theory of neutron stars and black holes, quantum field theory, and the interactions of cosmic rays.

In 1942, Oppenheimer was recruited to work on the Manhattan Project, and in 1943 was appointed director of the project's Los Alamos Laboratory in New Mexico, tasked with developing the first nuclear weapons, four years after the start of the German nuclear weapons program. His leadership and scientific expertise were instrumental in the project's success. On July 16, 1945, he was present at the Trinity test of the first atomic bomb. In August 1945, the weapons were used in the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in Japan, which remain the only use of nuclear weapons in an armed conflict.

In 1947, Oppenheimer became the director of the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, New Jersey, and chaired the influential General Advisory Committee of the newly created United States Atomic Energy Commission. He lobbied for international control of nuclear power, to avert nuclear proliferation and a nuclear arms race with the Soviet Union. He opposed the development of the hydrogen bomb during a 1949–1950 governmental debate on the question and subsequently took positions on defense-related issues that provoked the ire of some U.S. government and military factions. During the Second Red Scare, Oppenheimer's stances, together with past associations he had with people and organizations affiliated with the Communist Party USA, led to the revocation of his security clearance following a 1954 security hearing. Effectively stripped of his direct political influence, he continued to lecture, write, and work in physics. In 1963, President John F. Kennedy awarded him (and Lyndon B. Johnson presented him with) the Enrico Fermi Award as a gesture of political rehabilitation. In 2022, the U.S. government vacated its 1954 decision, saying that the process had been flawed.

All they hope that those involved in making “Oppenheimer” help raise awareness about the downwinders, who have not been added to the list of those covered by the federal government’s compensation program for people exposed to radiation.

Government officials chose the Trinity Test Site because it was remote, flat and had predictable winds. Due to the secret nature of the project, residents in surrounding areas were not warned.

The Tularosa Basin was home to a rural population that lived off the land by raising livestock and tending to gardens and farms. They drew water from cisterns and holding ponds. They had no idea that the fine ash that settled on everything in the days following the explosion was from the world’s first atomic blast.

The government initially tried to hide it, saying that an explosion at a munitions dump caused the rumble and bright light, which could be seen more than 160 miles (257 kilometers) away.

It wasn’t until the U.S. dropped bombs on Japan weeks later that New Mexico residents realized what they had witnessed.

I know I will be hated for this, but despite Nolan being my #1 film director, Oppenheimer made me feel conflicted and even triggered. The cinematography and audio was amazing on IMAX as expected, but it's probably his first film that I cannot just "feel it". (Semi-spoilers ahead)
First hour felt like a speedrun through the MC's life, with massive info. dump with crosscutting between 3+ timelines (Nolan did this a lot in his films, but it was carefully introduced in other films). It's kinda hard to relate when you don't really get any time to sit with the character in any of his life moment.
The best part was the middle, being the creation of the nuclear bomb blasted in Hiroshima. However, Oppenheimer's motivation was murky after a while. What I feel the film failed to do is claiming him to be"the most important figure in history of the world", but did not explore the impact of the disaster that nuclear power brings. The impact on Hiroshima were briefly mentioned and seen as illusions, trying to make us feel for the man who created this massive disaster without exploring his accountability enough. While moral conflict was there, but it's not at the center. Thr film might not praised Oppenheimer's actions, it still kinda raised him as an icon which is in bad taste for "me". I do love anti-heroes in fiction, but prasing real life figure doesn't feel right.
I do recommend people to watch Oppenheimer's real life speech on tge matter though, that was a man ridden with guilt.

Question for anyone who has seen the film, I'm genuinely curious: does it promote the myth that the US had to nuke Japan to end the war or are we finally getting an accurate historical depiction that the allies had agreed at the Potsdam conference that the Soviet Union would invade Japan even after Japan had tried to make peace negotiations and the US dropped the bombs as a power play against the Soviets?
_"The Japanese were ready to surrender, and it wasn't necessary to hit them with that awful thing. I hated to see our country be the first to use such a weapon."_
- Dwight D. Eisenhower, Supreme Commander of the Allied Expeditionary Force in Europe, later 34th president of the United States.

(There is a mention also that Japan actually already lost the war, even since Bombing of Tokyo, which is not really popular among historians, compared to the importance of Battle of Okinawa).

Oppenheimer is the true story of The American scientist J. Robert Oppenheimer and his role in the development of the atomic “Trinity” bomb in a small secret village called Oak Ridge,

Unbeknownst to the rest of the country, and to most of the people living in Oak Ridge, the so-called “Secret City” was at the epicenter of the Manhattan Project, the American-led effort to build an atomic bomb.

Oak Ridge can still be visited today, which is located near Knoxville, Tennessee, USA,


Manhattan Project

In 1942, the United States federal government chose the area as a site for developing materials for the Manhattan Project. Major general Leslie Groves, military head of the Manhattan Project, liked the area for several reasons. Its relatively low population made acquisition affordable, yet the area was accessible by highway and rail, and utilities such as water and electricity were readily available due to the recent completion of Norris Dam. Finally, the project location was established within a 17-mile-long (27 km) valley. This feature was linear and partitioned by several ridges, providing natural protection against the spread of disasters at the four major industrial plants—so they wouldn't blow up "like firecrackers on a string".

When the Governor of Tennessee Prentice Cooper was officially handed by a junior officer (a lieutenant) the July 1943 presidential proclamation making Oak Ridge a military district not subject to state control, he tore it up and refused to see the MED engineer, Lieutenant colonel James C. Marshall. The new District Engineer Kenneth Nichols had to placate him. Cooper came to see the project (except for the production facilities under construction) on November 3, 1943; and he appreciated the bourbon-laced punch served (although Anderson County was "dry"). House and dormitory accommodations at the Clinton Engineer Works (CEW) in Oak Ridge and Hanford Engineer Works in Washington State were basic, with coal rather than oil or electric furnaces. But they were of a higher standard than Director Groves wanted, and were better than at Los Alamos. Medical care was provided by Army doctors and hospitals, with civilians paying $2.50 per month ($5 for families) to the medical insurance fund.

The location and low population helped keep the town a secret, though the settlement grew from 3,000 to 3,750 in 1942 to about 75,000 by 1945. The K-25 uranium separation facility covered 44 acres (18 ha), then the largest building in the world. The name "Oak Ridge" was chosen for the settlement in 1943 from suggestions submitted by project employees. The name evoked the settlement's location along Black Oak Ridge, and officials thought the rural-sounding name "held outside curiosity to a minimum". The name wasn't formally adopted until 1949, and the site was referred to as the Clinton Engineer Works or CEW until then. All workers wore badges. The town was surrounded by guard towers and a fence with seven gates.

In October 1942, the United States Army Corps of Engineers began acquiring more than 60,000 acres (24,000 ha) in the Oak Ridge area for the United States' Manhattan Project. Unlike the earlier land acquisitions by the Tennessee Valley Authority for Norris Dam—which were still fresh on the minds of many Anderson Countians—the Corps' "declaration of taking" was much more swift and final. Many residents came home to find eviction notices tacked to their doors. Most were given six weeks to evacuate, although several had as little as two weeks. Some were forced out before they received compensation.

By March 1943, the COE had removed the area's earlier communities and established fences and checkpoints. Anderson County lost one-seventh of its land and $391,000 in annual property tax revenue. The manner by which the Oak Ridge area was acquired by the government created a tense, uneasy relationship between the Oak Ridge complex and the surrounding towns that lasted throughout the Manhattan Project. Although the area's original residents were allowed to be buried in existing cemeteries, every coffin was reportedly opened for inspection.

The Corps' Manhattan Engineer District (MED) managed the acquisition and clearing. The K-25, S-50, and Y-12 plants were each built in Oak Ridge to separate the fissile isotope uranium-235 from natural uranium, which consists almost entirely of the isotope uranium-238. During construction of the electromagnets required for the uranium separation process at the Y-12 site, a shortage of copper forced the MED to borrow 14,700 tons of silver bullion from the United States Treasury as a copper substitute in wire for the electromagnet coils. The X-10 site, now the site of Oak Ridge National Laboratory, was established as a pilot plant for production of plutonium using the Graphite Reactor.

Because of the large number of workers recruited to the area for the Manhattan Project, the Army planned a town for project workers at the eastern end of the valley. The time required for the project's completion caused the Army to opt for a relatively permanent establishment rather than an enormous camp.

The architecture firm Skidmore, Owings and Merrill (SOM) was contracted to provide the layout for the town and house designs. SOM Partner John O. Merrill moved to Tennessee to take charge of designing the secret buildings at Oak Ridge. He directed the creation of a town, which soon had 300 miles (480 km) of roads, 55 miles (89 km) of railroad track, ten schools, seven theaters, 17 restaurants and cafeterias, and 13 supermarkets. A library with 9,400 books, a symphony orchestra, sporting facilities, church services for 17 denominations, and a Fuller Brush Company salesman served the new city and its 75,000 residents. No airport was built, for security reasons. Prefabricated modular homes, apartments, and dormitories, many made from cemesto (bonded cement and asbestos) panels, were quickly erected. Streets were laid out in the manner of a "planned community".

The original streets included several main east-to-west roads, namely the Oak Ridge Turnpike, Tennessee Avenue, Pennsylvania Avenue, Hillside Road, Robertsville Road, and Outer Drive. North-to-south oriented streets connecting these main roads were designated "Avenues", and streets branching off from the avenues were designated "Roads", "Places", "Lanes", or "Circles". "Roads" connected two streets, while "Lanes" and "Places" were dead ends. The names of the main avenues generally progressed alphabetically from east to west (e.g., Alabama Avenue in the east, Vermont Avenue in the west), and the names of the smaller streets began with the same letter as the main avenue from which they started (e.g., streets connected to Florida Avenue began with "F").

Construction personnel swelled the wartime population of Oak Ridge to as much as 70,000. The dramatic population increase, and the secret nature of the project, meant chronic shortages of housing and supplies during the war years. The town was administered by Turner Construction Company through a subsidiary named the Roane-Anderson Company. For most residents, however, their "landlord" was known as "MSI" (Management Services, Inc.).

The news of the use of the first atomic bomb against Japan on August 6, 1945, was when it was first revealed to the people at Oak Ridge what they had been working on.

Julius Robert Oppenheimer was an American theoretical physicist and director of the Los Alamos Laboratory during World War II. He is often credited as the "father of the atomic bomb" for his role in organizing the Manhattan Project, the research and development undertaking that created the first nuclear weapons.

Born to German Jewish immigrants in New York City, Oppenheimer earned a bachelor's degree in chemistry from Harvard University in 1925 and a PhD in physics from the University of Göttingen in Germany in 1927. After research at other institutions, he joined the physics department at the University of California, Berkeley, where he became a full professor in 1936.

He made significant contributions to theoretical physics, including achievements in quantum mechanics and nuclear physics such as the Born–Oppenheimer approximation for molecular wave functions, work on the theory of electrons and positrons, the Oppenheimer–Phillips process in nuclear fusion, and the first prediction of quantum tunneling. With his students, he also made contributions to the theory of neutron stars and black holes, quantum field theory, and the interactions of cosmic rays.

In 1942, Oppenheimer was recruited to work on the Manhattan Project, and in 1943 was appointed director of the project's Los Alamos Laboratory in New Mexico, tasked with developing the first nuclear weapons, four years after the start of the German nuclear weapons program. His leadership and scientific expertise were instrumental in the project's success.

On July 16, 1945, he was present at the Trinity test of the first atomic bomb. In August 1945, the weapons were used in the bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in Japan, which remain the only use of nuclear weapons in an armed conflict.

In 1947, Oppenheimer became the director of the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, New Jersey, and chaired the influential General Advisory Committee of the newly created United States Atomic Energy Commission. He lobbied for international control of nuclear power, to avert nuclear proliferation and a nuclear arms race with the Soviet Union.

He opposed the development of the hydrogen bomb during a 1949–1950 governmental debate on the question and subsequently took positions on defense-related issues that provoked the ire of some U.S. government and military factions.

During the Second Red Scare, Oppenheimer's stances, together with past associations he had with people and organizations affiliated with the Communist Party USA, led to the revocation of his security clearance following a 1954 security hearing.

In 1963, President John F. Kennedy awarded him (and Lyndon B. Johnson presented him with) the Enrico Fermi Award as a gesture of political rehabilitation. In 2022, the U.S. government vacated its 1954 decision, saying that the process had been flawed.

This Thursday 🎥🍿 one of the most talked about films of the year, 'Oppenheimer', which was written and directed by director Christopher Nolan, will be released in theaters this Thursday. The film focuses on the life of the Jewish-American physicist, Robert Oppenheimer, and in particular on the impossible dilemma faced by the scientist who was the 'Father of the Atomic Bomb'⚛️. Get to know the story of the man who single-handedly changed the power relations between countries and made the world more advanced, but also dangerous and competitive >>

⚛️ Julius Robert Oppenheimer was born to wealthy Jewish parents in 1904, in New York. Young Robert was a gifted child who jumped between classes and was considered an outstanding student. In 1922 he began studying physics at Harvard University. He continued his studies while skipping between reputed universities and making a name for himself as a first-rate researcher in the field of quantum theory, which deals, among other things, with the study of the atom.

⚛️ It is told about Oppenheimer who was so devoted to his research that he ate little and smoked a lot. Later he was once quoted as saying that he needed physics more than friends. In between, Oppenheimer completed a doctorate in philosophy in 1927. All this by the age of 23, yes?

⚛️ In the thirties, Oppenheimer took part in research in astrophysics that gave validity to the idea of a black hole, a field that is still being researched today. In the midst of World War II, Oppenheimer was called to help a unique and secret program that President Roosevelt himself wanted to promote on behalf of the United States Army. The program was called the "Manhattan Project", a program that would change Oppenheimer's life. Although the program was under the strict management of Army General Leslie Groves, who served as the project manager on behalf of the United States Army, the research group that actually developed and designed the bombs was led by Oppenheimer, who served as the scientific director of the project at the Los Alamos, New Mexico laboratory.

⚛️ Oppenheimer gathered around him the best minds and the most respected scientists of that time. At one point he managed over 6,000 people, who for four years worked day and night to develop the bomb that would change the world. In July 1945, the first test of an atomic bomb that Oppenheimer called "Trinity" took place in New Mexico. Those who were present at the moment of the explosion and were amazed by the power and the great impact, later said that some of them burst into tears while some were completely paralyzed. They realized that this is the decisive moment when it is no longer possible to turn back the wheel.

⚛️ A month later, the bomb already became operational, when the United States Army dropped on Japan the first nuclear bomb in history, claiming the lives of thousands of Hiroshima residents. With the discovery and development of the bomb, Oppenheimer became famous. The face of the scientist who led to the development of the weapon that subdued the Japanese enemies and saved the lives of thousands of American soldiers, was emblazoned on the covers of magazines and newspapers, and he actually became a figure through which many fields of science were promoted. However, the dropping of the second bomb on Nagasaki caused Oppenheimer to question the decision to make another use of a bomb that he believed was unnecessary.

⚛️ Oppenheimer continued to engage in science even after the war. He won countless awards and numerous decorations thanks to his scientific contribution, a contribution that he himself withdrew from in the end.

⚛️ In 1966 he visited Israel, met Prime Minister David Ben Gurion and encouraged the developing science industry that the young country had.

⚛️ Oppenheimer's heavy smoking took a heavy toll and the Jewish scientist died in 1967 from throat cancer. Whether he meant to or not, Robert Oppenheimer may very well have been the most influential man of the 20th century.

If you think about it, they had to suspect that the shadow of nuclear weaponry in the world would in fact result in not the elimination of war, but rather the neverending threat of incomprehensibly devastating war. They did what they had to do against an enemy that absolutely could not be allowed to win the nuclear arms race, but indeed the result has been far from holding hands around the world and singing kumbaya. Every year since those days has seen nuclear nations endlessly taking turns threatening to annihilate the world. MAD has worked so far, sure, but the problem with the concept is in the resulting acronym; you can't ensure that every so-armed country will act indefinitely with the flawless sanity required to never ever let these things out of their cages. All it takes is one nuclear power that believes it has more to gain than to lose, and the desperation to act on that impulse. We've already been within a hair's breadth of it many times.

Also, a properly radioactive material doesn't sound like clickety click click on a Geiger counter. That's just the normal background. The good stuff sounds like "SHRRREEEEEEEEE" 🤣
